real_world_scenario.name: V1 Open-Workload Mixed Arrival Regression (LoRA 8-model sweep)
real_world_scenario.description: This suite re-runs the Chapter 5 Wave-Slice open-workload study on the V1 engine using all locally available non-Llama LoRA-capable models and locally cached open datasets. Each case mixes short conversational turns with long-context document tasks, orders short and long requests in a beneficiary-rich sequence, and assigns Poisson arrival times so that overlap and queueing are created by the workload rather than by a synthetic all-at-once burst.
why_real: UltraChat supplies short interactive prompts that resemble assistant turns and follow-up questions.
LongBench supplies long-context prompts such as report summarization and long-document QA.
Poisson arrivals provide a standard online-serving approximation for independent user arrivals with bursty overlap.
Keeping request counts fixed while sweeping arrival rate isolates traffic density as the main controlled stress variable.
Selecting locally cached model snapshots and datasets through configuration keeps the experiment reproducible without hardcoded model paths or dataset IDs in code.

method_definition.phase1: Use chunk-size control to return from long prefills to the scheduler boundary earlier.
method_definition.phase2: Use execution escape and scheduler-side priority promotion to reshape the next scheduled window.
method_definition.adaptation: Use runtime queue-pressure adaptation inside the scheduler; configured density only selects the workload, not the active chunk policy.
method_definition.pressure_scope_note: The active policy is chosen at each scheduler step from live queue length, waiting short count, wait urgency, long remaining tokens, and virtual-cap hit EMA.

densities.name: low
densities.reason: Requests still overlap, but sustained queueing is limited. This checks that the policy does not rely on artificial saturation to show gains.

densities.name: mid
densities.reason: This is the center operating band where short and long requests overlap frequently enough to expose meaningful tradeoffs without collapsing into full saturation.

densities.name: high
densities.reason: This density creates repeated head-of-line blocking and is the first regime where strong Phase I+II rescues should become clearly visible if the implementation is working.

densities.name: peak
densities.reason: This upper-band density keeps the queue pressure high enough to reveal robustness limits and wall-time regressions that would be hidden at lighter traffic.

adaptive_density_policy.pressure_formula: wall_pressure = weighted(queue, long_remaining, cap_hit, previous_wall); effective_pressure = wall_pressure * (1 - urgency_discount * short_urgency)
adaptive_density_policy.description: Runtime policy. Each scheduler step maps live pressure to chunk targets: short urgency pushes toward aggressive chunks, sustained wall pressure pushes toward conservative chunks.
